clues: Documented for over 4,000 years with earliest known descriptions of various types of this appearing in Akkadian texts around 2000BC
Due to intense stigma in the 19th century, specialized farms or 'colonies' were greated in Europe and the US to provide safe spaces for individuals affected by these to live and work
Initially thought to be contagious through breath or touch, Hippocrates challenged the notion that this was a manifestation of divine punishment or possession
Although commonly associated with a bacterial infection, interestingly, people with blood type A have a higher risk of developing this
Although relatively uncommon in the United States, rates remain high in East Asia leading to widespread national screening programs
In the 20th century, introduction of refrigeration reduced reliance on salted and smoked foods, lowering the risk of this disease in many countries
Although previously fatal before age 4, modern treatments of this have transformed this into a chronic disease
The nickname 'sixty-five roses' is also the unofficial symbol of the disease
This disease was described in European folkelore, 'the child will soon die whose brow tastes salty when kissed'
First isolated from raisins in 1747 by a German chemist, frequent monitoring of this is required for some patients
Also known as C6H12O6, the current term is derived from a greek word referring to sweet wine
Critical for creating ATP, the brain relies on this as its primary energy source
In the 17th century, the bark of the cinchona tree from South America was introduced as a powerful treatment for this as a natural source of quinine
This disease famously affected George Washington and Abraham Lincoln, but is no longer endemic in the United States
The name derives from Italian for 'spoiled air' or 'bad air' reflecting the belief that this disease was caused by vapors from swamps
